name: Deontological Rule-Based Safety
title: EF-02 — Deontological Rule-Based Safety
foundation: Fixed moral rules hold regardless of outcome. The AV has an inviolable duty never to be the proximate cause of harm. Safety obligations are not trade-offs — they are categorical constraints. The Responsibility-Sensitive Safety (RSS) model formalises this: an AV that follows RSS rules can never be held causally responsible for a collision.
decision_logic: Enforce hard safety constraints derived from RSS: maintain safe longitudinal distance d_min = v_r * rho + (v_r^2 / 2*a_min) - (v_f^2 / 2*a_max), respect lateral safe gaps, obey right-of-way rules and speed limits. Any candidate trajectory that violates a hard constraint is categorically rejected regardless of its expected utility or risk score. Only trajectories within the safe envelope are evaluated further. If no trajectory satisfies all constraints, the AV must perform a proper response (emergency braking) rather than choose the lesser harm.
pros: Predictable and consistent — same rules apply in every scenario
Legally aligned — compatible with existing traffic law and liability frameworks
Does not discriminate between individuals based on group membership or numbers
Formally verifiable — safety guarantees can be mathematically proven
Appropriate for routine non-dilemma driving which covers the vast majority of cases
Clear responsibility attribution — RSS compliance means the AV cannot be at fault
cons: Inflexible in genuine edge cases — may produce no valid action in unavoidable dilemmas
Cannot weigh competing harms when all available options violate some rule
Rules may conflict across jurisdictions requiring jurisdiction-specific parameterisation
Does not account for contextual moral nuance or vulnerability of individuals
Binary outcome — a trajectory either passes or fails, no gradient for optimisation
best_fit_scenarios: routine non-dilemma driving which constitutes the vast majority of AV operation
legally regulated environments with clear right-of-way rules
scenarios where responsibility attribution and legal liability matter
highway scenarios with well-defined lane discipline and following distance rules
any scenario where a safe trajectory exists that avoids all conflicts
poor_fit_scenarios: genuine unavoidable collision dilemmas where all options cause harm
novel scenarios not covered by existing traffic rules
scenarios requiring comparison between two harmful outcomes
emergency situations where rule violation may reduce overall harm
tradeoffs: vs Utilitarian (EF-01): rejects any harm-minimising action that violates a hard rule even if it would reduce total expected harm. vs Maximin (EF-03): does not consider the welfare of the worst-off party — if protecting the most vulnerable requires a rule violation, EF-02 disallows it. vs Ethics of Risk (EF-04): acts as a hard filter before EF-04 weighted optimisation — EF-02 defines the feasible set, EF-04 selects within it. vs EVT (EF-05): rule-based, not outcome-based — social valence of road user types is irrelevant to rule compliance.
key_parameters: safe_distance_min d_min based on reaction time rho and braking bounds
source_papers: Naumann et al. (2021) — On Responsibility Sensitive Safety in Car-following Situations
Geisslinger et al. (2023) — An Ethical Trajectory Planning Algorithm for Autonomous Vehicles
